1: laYHWH hāʾāreṣ ûmĕlōʾāh tēbēl wĕyōšĕbê bāh (laYHWH hāʾāreṣ ûmĕlōʾāh tēḇēl wĕyōšĕḇê ḇāh): 'The earth is the LORD's and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell in it.' The psalm opens with a creation-sovereignty declaration that Paul cites in 1 Cor 10:26 regarding the lawfulness of eating food offered to idols — if the earth belongs to YHWH, no corner of it is excluded from his lordship. The double statement — hāʾāreṣ ûmĕlōʾāh ('the earth and its fullness') and tēbēl wĕyōšĕbê bāh ('the world and those who dwell in it') — encompasses both the land and its inhabitants. The definite laYHWH ('to/belonging to the LORD') is exclusive ownership.
2: kî hûʾ ʿal-yammîm yĕsādāh (kî hûʾ ʿal-yammîm yĕsāḏāh): 'for he has founded it upon the seas.' The verb yāsad (piel, 'to found, establish') describes YHWH as creator and sustainer of the cosmic order. The phrase ʿal-yammîm yĕsādāh wĕʿal-nĕhārôt yĕkônĕnehā, 'he has established it upon the rivers,' draws on the ancient cosmology of the earth founded above primordial waters (Gen 1:9-10; Ps 136:6). The founding of the earth on the seas is the ground of YHWH's ownership: the creator owns the creation. This cosmological foundation makes the entrance-liturgy of vv.3-10 coherent — only the creator's anointed can stand on this holy mountain.
3: mî yāʿăleh bĕhar YHWH ûmî yāqûm bimqôm qodšô (mî yāʿăleh ḇĕhar YHWH ûmî yāqûm bimqôm qodšô): 'Who shall ascend the hill of the LORD? And who shall stand in his holy place?' The double rhetorical question mirrors Ps 15:1. The verb ʿālâ ('to go up, ascend') is the pilgrimage term (cf. ʿaliyyâ to Jerusalem); qûm bimqôm qodšô ('stand in his holy place') is the liturgical term for entering the sanctuary precinct and remaining. The answer given in v.4 is moral, not ritual — not ceremonial purity but ethical integrity qualifies one to ascend.
4: nĕqî kappayim ûbar lēbāb (nĕqî ḵappayim ûḇar lēḇāḇ): 'He who has clean hands and a pure heart.' The binary of hands and heart — outward act and inward disposition — defines integrity comprehensively. nĕqî kappayim ('clean of hands') denotes innocent of bloodshed and unethical dealings; bar lēbāb ('pure/clear of heart') denotes undivided loyalty. Matt 5:8 ('blessed are the pure in heart') echoes the lēb bārûr. The next clause: ʾăšer lōʾ nāśāʾ laššāwĕʾ napšô wĕlōʾ nišbaʿ lĕmirmâ, 'who does not lift up his soul to what is false, and does not swear deceitfully' — adds truthfulness in oath-taking (cf. Ps 15:4) as a second criterion.
5: yiśśāʾ bĕrākâ mēʾēt YHWH ûṣĕdāqâ mēʾĕlōhê yišʿô (yiśśāʾ ḇĕrāḵâ mēʾēṯ YHWH ûṣĕḏāqâ mēʾĕlōhê yišʿô): 'He will receive blessing from the LORD and righteousness from the God of his salvation.' The one who qualifies (v.4) receives bĕrākâ ('blessing') and ṣĕdāqâ ('righteousness') — both from YHWH. The ṣĕdāqâ here is not mere ethical standing but the verdict of righteousness from the divine judge: to be declared righteous by the God who saves. The phrase ʾĕlōhê yišʿô ('the God of his salvation') personalizes it — this is 'my God who saves.'
6: zeh dôr dōrĕšāyw mĕbaqqĕšê pānêkā yaʿăqōb (zeh dôr dōrĕšāyw mĕbaqqĕšê p̄āneḵā yaʿăqōḇ): 'Such is the generation of those who seek him, who seek your face, O Jacob.' The dôr ('generation') of seekers is the community defined not by birth but by orientation — those who seek (dāraš) YHWH and seek his face (bāqaš pānêkā). The address 'O Jacob' (Selah) is unusual — it may be a gloss or liturgical call. The generation of seekers constitutes the people of God in each era: the covenant community is defined by the pattern of life described in v.4, not ethnic origin.
7: śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem wĕhinnāśĕʾû pitḥê ʿôlām wĕyābôʾ melek hakkābôd (śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem wĕhinnāśĕʾû piṯḥê ʿôlām wĕyāḇôʾ meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ): 'Lift up your heads, O gates! And be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in.' The imperatives — śĕʾû ('lift up!') and hinnāśĕʾû ('be lifted up!') — personify the gates as figures bowing before a greater king. The phrase melek hakkābôd ('the King of glory') — YHWH as the kābôd-bearing sovereign — is the psalm's climactic title. The setting was likely the procession of the ark into Jerusalem (2 Sam 6) or an annual liturgical re-enactment. The NT applies the entry imagery to the ascension of Christ through the heavenly gates (Heb 1:6; Ps 47:5).
8: mî zeh melek hakkābôd YHWH ʿizzûz wĕgibbôr (mî zeh meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ YHWH ʿizzûz wĕḡibbôr): 'Who is this King of glory? The LORD, strong and mighty.' The dialogue format — question (v.8a) / answer (v.8b) — may reflect a liturgical antiphony between two groups: those outside the gate asking, those inside answering. The divine title YHWH ʿizzûz wĕgibbôr YHWH gibbôr milḥāmâ, 'the LORD, strong and mighty, the LORD, mighty in battle,' invokes the divine warrior tradition (Exod 15:3; Isa 42:13). YHWH's glory is specifically the glory of a warrior who wins battles — a counter-claim against any military power that oppresses Israel.
9: śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem ûśĕʾû pitḥê ʿôlām (śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem ûśĕʾû piṯḥê ʿôlām): 'Lift up your heads, O gates! Lift them up, O ancient doors.' The repetition of the summons (cf. v.7) with slight variation — the second half reads ûśĕʾû rather than wĕhinnāśĕʾû — creates the intensification of liturgical repetition. The repetition is not redundancy but escalation: the call goes out a second time, more urgent. The doors (pitḥê ʿôlām, 'eternal doors') are designated by their antiquity — they have witnessed ages of divine activity; now they open for the greatest entrance.
10: mî hûʾ zeh melek hakkābôd YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt hûʾ melek hakkābôd (mî hûʾ zeh meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôṯ hûʾ meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ): 'Who is this King of glory? The LORD of hosts, he is the King of glory!' The final identification YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ('LORD of hosts/armies') is the most comprehensive divine title in the Psalter — YHWH as the commander of the heavenly armies (the stars, angels, and cosmic forces). The declaration hûʾ melek hakkābôd ('he is the King of glory') is the psalm's answer to the question it has posed throughout: the one who owns the earth (v.1), who founded it (v.2), whose holy mountain is approached only by the ethically qualified (vv.3-6), who enters in military triumph (vv.7-9) — this one is the LORD of hosts. Selah closes the psalm with a meditative pause on the name.
